title: Phenomenon 11
wikitext:
Due to the extremely high frequency, varying properties and forms, and often inconsistent dangers of '''Instability''', a proper classification is impossible to give to this phenomenon. Be prepared for anything in your travels throughout the Backrooms.
==Overview==__NOTOC__ {{Blurimage|file=unstable3.png|caption=A recovered photograph of [[Level 3]], namely, an unstable area of the aforementioned level.}} Throughout the confines of the Backrooms, '''Phenomenon 11 (Instability)''' is perhaps one of the most common phenomena one will encounter. Manifesting as a general lack of coherent physical laws, Instability runs rampant in the Backrooms throughout its many levels, changing the landscape of this dimension for better or worse. Although this anomaly's function is rather simple when one analyzes its barest components, it has wide reaching consequences; due to its existence, all those who wish to study or traverse the Backrooms are reminded of one thing: nothing is ever truly consistent within this dimension. ==Further Description== By their very nature, instances of instability are highly varied in manifestation; however, all instances of Phenomenon 11 manifest as "true" anomalies. Most anomalous phenomena within the Backrooms take the form of scientific systems incompatible with the conventional laws of reality, or constructs that although unexplainable by anomalous or non-anomalous science, are internally consistent. In contrast, the anomalies created by Instability (i.e "true" anomalies) largely lack internal consistency, shifting methods and effects on a whim. The visual markers of instability range from subtle to extremely obvious. Often, one can vaguely ascertain slight differences in stability by how "off" an area appears; this is likely a preconscious The memories or feelings that are not part of one's immediate awareness but that can be recalled through conscious effort. psychological reaction to the subtle visual abnormalities caused by unstable objects. With more obvious cases of instability, visual cues of this phenomenon are more varied and obvious: they often include anomalous reactions with light (wherein light will be bent in unnatural ways, or light sources will give off more or less illumination than they usually should), small fragments of matter blinking in and out of existence to cause a visual effect reminiscent of digital glitches, and sound working differently than it should. ==Morrison Stability Scale== In order to properly ascertain the stability of a given level, [[The Kauer Research Organization]] has devised a system to measure instability known as the '''Morrison Stability Scale'''. Due to the [[Extended Almond Water Sub-Item Dossier (Collaborative)|significant reactivity]] of [[Object 1|Almond Water]], the effects of instability often affect its composition greatly; therefore, by measuring how much of the original Almond Water remains after exposed to a level after a full day of exposure (on a scale of 1-10), one can by proxy measure how unstable a given level isOf course, there is a significant margin of error while using the Morrison Stability Scale; repeated tests are often necessary to accurately measure the stability of a given area.. {{ScaleBar|min=0|max=10|minText=Perfect Stability|maxText=Null Stability|divs=9|title=
Morrison Stability Scale
}} Below is a list of all formal classifications of the Morrison Stability Scale, as well as common phenomena which correlate with them. Note that these effects are rarely universal; manifestations will wildly vary between each area. ===0 (Perfect Stability)=== ''Examples: [[Uncharted Territory]] (theorized)'' Although no instances of it are confirmed to exist, Perfect Stability is still undergoing study as a theoretical phenomenon; similarly to [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_zero absolute zero] in thermodynamics, reaching this point of stability would be impossible for anything which did not originate in this state. In an environment with perfect stability, breaking or contradicting the space's local laws of physics would be completely impossible. Some scientists within the Backrooms argue that [[Uncharted Territory]] would in fact be an instance of this, due to its status of being outside existence making it devoid of any instability. ===0.1-0.5 (Baseline)=== ''Examples: [[The Frontrooms]], [[Level 399]]'' Being either familiar or very unusual depending on whether one was born in the Backrooms, Baseline Stability is the "standard" level of instability in normal reality. Within our reality, the majority of anomalous phenomena observed in other dimensions are largely unable to occur; despite this, minor disturbances in reality can rarely happenThe most prominent example being the occurrence of one no-clipping to the Backrooms in the first place.. ===0.5-2.5 (Very Stable) === ''Examples: [[Level 10]], [[Level 20]]'' At the threshold of "Very Stable" stability levels, the levels of anomalous phenomena specific to instability are similar in scope to baseline reality; however, the aforementioned minor disturbances in reality happen significantly more often, thus making occurrences like no-clipping much more reliable to perform. Additionally, at the microscopic level, small molecules of matter are observed to pop in and out of existence, possibly resulting in spontaneous chemical reactions. ===2.6-5.0 (Stable)=== ''Examples: [[Level 1]], [[Level 483]]'' At levels ranging from 2.5 to 5.0 on the Morrison Stability Scale, one has reached the average stability for most levels in the Backrooms. At this range, wanderers in the area will begin to feel that something is notably "off" about the space. Minor "glitches" in the laws of physics are near constant; constants such as the local [https://backrooms.fandom.com/wiki/Uncharted_Territory#Entropics entropic preference], results of chemical reactions, gravity, mass, [[Phenomenon 57|the speed of time]], and force will begin to constantly fluctuate within mild parameters. Although these effects are usually quite benign physically, their psychological effects can prove potent; it as at this point of instability that [[Phenomenon 35|Roth Syndrome]] begins to be a significant concern, and as mentioned prior, the numerous minor anomalies can prove both unnerving and disorienting. Combined, these factors often cause wanderers to experience feelings of paranoia or anxiety. ===5.1-7.5 (Unstable)=== ''Examples: [[Level 128]], [[Level 3]]'' At this point, the more obvious signs of instability will begin to manifest, with areas which appear visually "glitched" being rather common. The mild inconsistences in basic laws of physics will become even more common, as well as more pronounced; those who enter these area often report their experience being "confusingly dreamlike, yet terrifyingly lucid". Basic physical interactions will prove unreliable at best, and [[Phenomenon 35|Roth Syndrome]] will be a major threat; unless you intend to inhabit them for an extended period, make excursions into unstable levels quick and efficient. === 7.6-9.5 (Very Unstable)=== ''Examples: [[Level 111]], [[The Corrupted Instability]]'' Levels over 7.5 on the Morrison Stability Scale will often actively begin to impede survival with their instability. Errors in basic logic often occur at this extent, and wanderers themselves will begin to be affected by the "glitches" within the level, often experiencing a painful sensation comparable to being pricked with pin and needles. [[Object 32|Corruptium]] is a common sight in these sorts of levels, and physical constants are predominately random and disjointed. Travel in these spaces is not recommended, as it poses a large risk of injury and illness due to the instability affecting one's body. ===9.5-9.9 (Fragmented)=== ''Examples: [[The Broken]]'' Little to no coherency in reality can be expected from these spaces. Matter, space, and time will warp and bend to near incomprehensible states; most who enter leave with severe injuries and numerous anomalous afflictions at best. Avoid these at all costs; the Broken and its fragments were not meant for mankind. ===10 (Null Stability)=== ''Examples: [[Uncharted Territory]] (theorized)'' At a perfect 10 on the Morrison Stability Scale, consistency in physical laws and reality would be entirely nonexistent; such a space would be a nexus of chaos and disorder, making the Broken look tame by comparison. Conversely to those who believe that [[Uncharted Territory]] is an example of a perfectly stable space, other scientists speculate that due to Uncharted Territory forbidding existence within its bounds, it would lack consistency in physical laws, thus being an example of Null Stability. However, due to the inaccessibility of any space outside of [[The Void]], these notions are a proverbial [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell's_teapot Russell's teapot], unable to be proven or disproven. {{Footnotes}}
